reason: Recovery is usable but strained: training readiness was moderate at 50, HRV was balanced but right at the low edge of your personal range, sleep score was fair, stress averaged 37, and Body Battery ended low after a solid step day plus a hike.
action: Make today a recovery-biased day: easy walk or mobility, hydration/electrolytes, and another high-protein meal. If you train, keep it Zone 2 or light strength.
sleep: 7h 17m • score 60 / Fair • deep 22% • REM 21% • avg sleep stress 39
hrv: 36 ms overnight • Balanced • personal balanced range 36–53 ms • weekly avg 45 ms
stress: Avg 37 • max 91 • Garmin qualifier STRESSFUL • 22.7% rest, 43.5% stress
training: Readiness 50 / Moderate • acute load 290 • ACWR 0.9 optimal • VO2 max 43 • high-aerobic shortage
vitals: Resting HR 63 vs 7-day avg 60 • SpO2 avg 95%, low 90 • respiration latest 16/min
weeklySummary: Last 7 days: 43,162 steps across available complete days; step goal hit on Jun 3 and nearly hit Jun 1. Intensity minutes are already 245 / 150 for the week.
footerNote: Generated by Hermes from Garmin + nutrition logs. Public granular view scheduled to roll back July 3, 2026.

metricCards.label: Recovery decision
metricCards.title: Training readiness
metricCards.summary: Best shown as a 0–100 readiness dial/card because it combines sleep, HRV, recovery time, load, and stress history.

metricCards.label: Autonomic recovery
metricCards.title: HRV vs baseline
metricCards.summary: Best shown against your personal baseline band, not compared to other people. Yours is balanced, but at the low edge.

metricCards.label: Sleep quality
metricCards.title: Sleep score
metricCards.summary: Best shown as score + duration + restorative breakdown; duration was fine, stress made it less restorative.

metricCards.label: Energy budget
metricCards.title: Body Battery
metricCards.summary: Best shown as wake → current plus charge/drain; this tells whether the day is depleting faster than you recovered.

metricCards.label: Physiological load
metricCards.title: Stress avg
metricCards.summary: Best shown as average plus rest/stress split. Garmin treats under 25 as rest/recovery and above 25 as sympathetic load.

metricCards.label: Movement output
metricCards.title: Steps
metricCards.summary: Best shown against the daily goal, with distance and active calories as context.

metricCards.label: Weekly activity
metricCards.title: Intensity minutes
metricCards.summary: High-value because it shows whether weekly cardiovascular activity is sufficient, not just whether today was active.

metricCards.label: Training context
metricCards.title: Load + VO2
metricCards.summary: High-value add: training load and VO2 max explain fitness direction; current load is optimal but high-aerobic work is short.

metricCards.label: Nutrition adherence
metricCards.title: Protein logged
metricCards.summary: Best shown as calories/protein completion, but only breakfast is logged so far. More meal logging makes this much more useful.

metricCards.label: Resting vitals
metricCards.title: RHR
metricCards.summary: High-value add: resting HR, SpO2, and respiration can flag recovery strain, illness, altitude, or sleep disruption.

focus: Today: recovery-biased activity unless you feel unusually good.
Improve dashboard value by logging lunch/dinner/water so nutrition is not breakfast-only.
Experiment metric set: readiness, HRV baseline, sleep score, Body Battery, stress split, activity, intensity minutes, training load, VO2 max, nutrition, and resting vitals.
Rollback to a sanitized public view is scheduled for July 3, 2026.
displayNotes: Use cards for scanability on iPhone: one primary number, status pill, and 2–3 context details.
Use baseline/range visualization for HRV because personal baseline matters more than population comparison.
Use progress bars for goal-based metrics: readiness, sleep score, Body Battery, steps, intensity minutes, calories/protein.
Use 7-day trends for movement and resting metrics so one bad day does not dominate the interpretation.
Add high-value context beyond the original dashboard: training readiness, acute load/ACWR, VO2 max, resting HR, SpO2, respiration, intensity minutes, and nutrition adherence.
